notes: Desktop processor using GHz equivalency model numbering (e.g. 2000+ = 1.667GHz actual); Palomino and Thoroughbred cores; model numbers ranged from 1500+ to 3200+
NetBurst architecture desktop processor at 2.0GHz; 180nm process; the specific model against which AMD calibrated its GHz equivalency benchmarks
Improved P4 variant with larger cache and 130nm process; 2.0A GHz Northwood delivered significantly better performance than Willamette at same clock speed; invalidated AMD equivalency claims
System-level benchmark measuring office productivity and content creation; counted for over one-third of AMD's total equivalency score; required public disclosure of results
Industry-standard compute-intensive processor benchmark; Aberdeen recommended as more appropriate processor-only benchmark than application-level tests
3D graphics benchmark using DirectX 8.1; used in AMD's entertainment/gaming benchmark suite
System-level benchmark measuring PC performance using real applications; replaced by Winstone 2002 during analysis period; criticized for measuring system I/O rather than processor performance
Older graphics API used by Winbench 2000 for 3D testing; obsolete on Windows XP which shipped with DirectX 8.1; using it in benchmarks was identified as a methodology flaw
Graphics API included with Windows XP; tested via 3DMark 2001; represented current gaming graphics standard at time of analysis
Desktop OS launched October 2001; all benchmark tests ran on Windows XP; subsequent patches created benchmark instability over time
